stem: An architect must choose between fine-tuning and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) so an assistant can answer from a large, frequently-updated internal knowledge base. Which is the better fit and why?
options: Fine-tuning, because it always beats RAG
RAG, because it grounds answers in current, retrievable sources and updates without retraining
Neither; put the whole knowledge base in every prompt permanently
Fine-tuning, to avoid ever citing sources
rationale: RAG suits large, changing knowledge because content is retrieved at query time and can be updated without retraining, and answers can cite sources. Stuffing everything into every prompt is infeasible and costly.

stem: Which are sound architectural practices for a production RAG system on Claude? (Select TWO.)
options: Chunk and index sources, retrieve the most relevant context per query, and pass it with the question
Cache large stable instructions/context with prompt caching to cut cost and latency
Send the entire corpus in every request
Disable evaluation to ship faster
rationale: Effective RAG retrieves only relevant chunks per query; prompt caching reduces cost/latency for stable prefixes. Sending the whole corpus every time and skipping evals are anti-patterns.

stem: A complex analytical task needs the strongest reasoning available and can tolerate higher latency. The architect should:
options: Use the smallest model and raise temperature
Select the most capable model and consider enabling extended thinking for the hardest steps
Avoid the API and answer from training data
Set max_tokens to 1
rationale: Match the model to task difficulty: the most capable model, optionally with extended thinking, fits hard reasoning tasks where latency is acceptable.

stem: For a long-context workload with a large, stable system prompt reused across many calls, which technique most reduces cost and latency?
options: Prompt caching of the stable prefix
Increasing temperature
Sending fewer messages by dropping needed context
Switching off streaming
rationale: Prompt caching reuses the stable prefix across requests, cutting cost and latency without dropping needed context.

stem: A high-volume, latency-tolerant nightly job scores millions of records. Which integration pattern is most cost-effective?
options: Synchronous real-time calls one at a time
The Message Batches API for asynchronous bulk processing at reduced cost
Streaming each request to a browser
Fine-tuning a model per record
rationale: Large asynchronous workloads that tolerate delay are the ideal use of the Message Batches API, which processes them at reduced cost.

stem: An enterprise wants to run Claude within its existing AWS data boundary. Which integration option supports this?
options: Claude is only available via the first-party API
Claude models available through Amazon Bedrock (and Google Vertex AI) let teams deploy within their cloud environment
The model must be downloaded and self-hosted on-prem
There is no enterprise deployment option
rationale: Claude is offered through Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI in addition to the first-party API, enabling deployment within a team's existing cloud/governance boundary.

stem: Before and after a prompt/model change in production, the most rigorous way to know if quality improved is to:
options: Read a few outputs and decide by feel
Run an evaluation on a representative, versioned test set with graded criteria and compare scores
Ship and wait for complaints
Compare only token costs
rationale: A representative, versioned eval with graded criteria provides objective before/after comparison. Anecdotes, complaint-driven QA, and cost-only comparisons don't measure quality.

stem: Which is an appropriate use of an LLM-as-judge in an evaluation pipeline?
options: Replacing all human review permanently with no calibration
Scoring outputs against an explicit rubric to scale grading, calibrated against human labels
Grading with no criteria at all
Judging with the same prompt that generated the output, unreviewed
rationale: LLM-as-judge scales grading when driven by an explicit rubric and calibrated against human labels. It should not run rubric-free or entirely uncalibrated.

stem: For a solution handling regulated personal and health data (e.g., GDPR/HIPAA), which governance practices are appropriate? (Select TWO.)
options: Minimize and control what sensitive data is sent, and choose a deployment/data-handling posture that meets compliance requirements
Maintain human oversight and audit logging for consequential decisions
Send all raw PII to the model by default for convenience
Disable logging entirely so nothing is traceable
rationale: Data minimization, a compliant deployment posture, human oversight, and audit trails are core governance controls for regulated data. Defaulting to sending all PII or removing traceability violates governance.

stem: An untrusted-input feature could be targeted by prompt injection to misuse tools. Which mitigation set is most appropriate?
options: Trust all input and grant the agent broad permissions
Delimit and treat untrusted content as data, constrain tool permissions to least privilege, and add guardrails/monitoring
Only increase max_tokens
Rely solely on the model refusing
rationale: Defense-in-depth against injection: isolate untrusted content, apply least-privilege tool permissions, and add guardrails and monitoring. Broad trust or relying solely on refusals is insufficient.

stem: When presenting a Claude solution's readiness to non-technical executives, the most effective communication focuses on:
options: Raw benchmark token counts only
Business outcomes, risks and mitigations, evaluation results, and cost/rollout plan in stakeholder-relevant terms
Low-level API parameters
A promise that the system is perfect
rationale: Executive communication should translate technical work into outcomes, risks/mitigations, evidence (evals), and a cost/rollout plan — not raw parameters or overpromises.

stem: Which lifecycle practice best sustains quality after launch as usage and content evolve?
options: Freeze the prompt and never revisit it
Continuously monitor outputs and costs, re-run evals on changes, and iterate with versioned prompts/content
Rely on the initial launch tests indefinitely
Remove monitoring once it works once
rationale: Ongoing monitoring, regression evals on changes, and versioned iteration keep a deployed system healthy as inputs drift. Freezing or dropping monitoring lets quality decay silently.

stem: To improve developer productivity and operational enablement across teams adopting Claude, an effective step is to:
options: Let every team reinvent prompts and tooling independently
Provide shared, reusable assets (prompt libraries, evals, MCP servers, CLAUDE.md conventions) and enablement docs
Restrict access so only one person can build
Prohibit evaluation to move faster
rationale: Operational enablement scales through shared reusable assets and documented conventions, reducing duplication and raising baseline quality. Siloing or banning evals hurts productivity and reliability.

stem: A design must degrade gracefully if the model or a tool is temporarily unavailable. Which architectural practice supports this?
options: Assume every call always succeeds
Add timeouts, retries with backoff, fallbacks, and structured error handling around model and tool calls
Remove all error handling to simplify code
Cache nothing and retry infinitely with no limit
rationale: Resilient architectures use timeouts, bounded retries with backoff, fallbacks, and structured error handling. Assuming success or unbounded retries creates fragility and cascading failures.

stem: An integration must call Claude and several internal systems as tools. Which approach keeps the integration maintainable and standardized?
options: Bespoke one-off glue code per system with no shared contract
Expose internal systems via MCP servers with well-scoped tools/resources the agent can reuse
Embed each system's credentials in the prompt
Fine-tune a model per internal system
rationale: MCP provides a standardized way to expose internal systems as reusable tools/resources, improving maintainability over bespoke glue or credential-in-prompt hacks.

stem: Latency in a user-facing feature is too high. Which optimization is most likely to help without lowering answer quality?
options: Prompt caching stable prefixes and right-sizing the model to the task, validated by an eval
Raising temperature
Removing all system instructions
Always using the largest model for every request
rationale: Caching stable prefixes and choosing an appropriately sized model (verified by eval) reduce latency while holding quality. Temperature doesn't reduce latency, stripping instructions harms quality, and always maxing model size raises latency/cost.
